title: Scattering-Induced Loss in Ferroelectric Photonic Devices
authors: Jonah Townsend, Enzo Conceição Picinini, Rogério de Sousa
abstract: Ferroelectric materials have colossal optical nonlinearities, but their integration into quantum photonic chips is made challenging by the additional loss mechanisms that they introduce. Here we present a perturbative theory that expresses non-absorptive (elastic) photon scattering-induced loss as a functional of a general spectral density for spatial fluctuations of electric permittivity. We apply the theory to calculations of attenuation coefficients $α$ in slab waveguides in order to compare two distinct loss mechanisms: Interface roughness and ferroelectric domain disorder. our theory can account for realistic roughness without special symmetry considerations, and it demonstrates how to use electron microscoopy images of ferroelectric domains to obtain explicit numerical predictions for $α$. Loss is maximum when the mean domain length is comparable to the wavelength of light (Mie regime), indicating that, for telecom wavelengths, sub-micron domains (Rayleigh regime) or single domain waveguides provide equivalent strategies for reducing loss.
summary: The paper develops a theory to quantify photon loss in ferroelectric waveguides caused by interface roughness and domain disorder. It demonstrates that loss peaks when domain sizes match the wavelength and suggests using sub-micron domains to enable low-loss quantum photonic devices.
main_problem: Addressing the high optical loss in ferroelectric photonic devices, specifically Barium Titanate (BTO) waveguides, which hinders their integration into quantum photonic chips and the quantum internet.
main_result: The study identifies that loss is maximized in the Mie regime (domain size ~ wavelength) and provides a strategy to reduce loss by operating in the Rayleigh regime (sub-micron domains) or using single-domain waveguides.
method: A perturbative electrodynamical theory using the sourceless Ampere-Maxwell law to treat permittivity fluctuations as effective volume currents, integrated with electron microscopy data.
model_or_system: Barium Titanate (BTO) on Silicon Dioxide (SiO2) slab waveguides, featuring spatial fluctuations in electric permittivity due to interface roughness and ferroelectric domain disorder.
important_parameters: Domain length scales (L), wavelength of light (lambda), refractive indices of core and cladding, Mie regime, and Rayleigh regime.
main_assumptions: Neglect of near-field effects in unguided mode loss calculation; use of a free-space approximation for unguided modes; and a simplified model for domain loss using only primary peaks.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a waveguide diagram with roughness and domain distribution; Figure 2 compares 1D and 2D roughness models; Figure 3 displays spectral density of fluctuations; Figure 4 illustrates the transition between Mie and Rayleigh regimes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of loss in BTO waveguides, presents a perturbative theory for scattering, applies the theory to interface roughness and domain disorder, compares 1D and 2D models, and concludes with design implications for low-loss devices.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for quantum optics and quantum networking as it provides a theoretical framework for minimizing photon loss in high-nonlinearity materials essential for scalable quantum photonic integrated circuits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Diagram of a barium titanate (BTO) on silicon dioxide (SiO2) dielectric waveguide, where z is the direction of propagation, depicting interface roughness and stochastic distribution of domains with orthogonal optical axes. The BTO unit cell displayed in the top left corner shows the a- and c-axes, as well as the polarization P as a result of the displaced titanium ion.

FIG. 2. Attenuation αδh for the lowest TE mode18 of a BTO planar slab waveguide due to roughness on the bottom BTO/SiO2 interface. (a) αδh vs. vacuum wavelength λ0 for roughness correlation Lc = 0.1µm and standard deviation σh = 2nm.19 (b) αδh vs. Lc for λ0 = 1.55µm and σh = 2nm. Other parameters shown in Table I. We compare the 1D roughness model (height ﬂuctuations δh ≡δh(z), with roughness along propagation direction z only) to the more realistic 2D roughness model with δh ≡δh(y,z). For 1D, we show that our model falls right in-between the coupled mode theory10 and Payne-Lacey11 models. In contrast, our more realistic 2D model leads to attenuation that is systematically smaller than all...

FIG. 3. Spectral density ˜Cδε(q∥) for dielectric constant ﬂuctuations due to ferroelectric domains in BTO, for (a) slow cooled and (b) fast cooled growth conditions.14 The spectral densities were obtained by mapping electron microscopy data (Fig. 1c in 14) to ε and taking the Fourier transform. Each ﬁgure shows two large primary peaks at ±Q0 that demonstrate nearly stripe order for the domains.

Figure 4(b) presents the attenuation as a function of ¯L for different values of Lc/¯L, for telecom λ0 = 1.55µm. The at- tenuation is seen to have a broad maximum across a band of values in the Mie regime with λ0 ∼¯L. This again can be un- derstood from the change in momentum supplied by ˜Cδε(∆k). In the limit Lc →∞, ˜Cδε(∆k) is sharply peaked at ∆k = ±Q0 (the dominant domain pattern), and the angular integration in Eq. (3) leads to a 1/|cosφ| singularity that appears when we integrate a sharply peaked function that depends on sinφ. FIG. 4. Attenuation αδε in a single-mode BTO planar waveguide due to ferroelectric domains, using the ﬁelds of the lowest order TE guided mode.18 (a) Calculated αδε vs. λ0 using Eq. (3). The slow and fast cooled αδε assumed spectral density ˜Cδε(q∥) directly interpolated from the values of Fig. 3(a,b). These are seen to be in good agreement with the simple model Eq. (7) with ¯L = 10 nm and Lc = 100 nm, that neglect domain wall higher harmonics. (b) αδε vs. ¯L for λ0 = 1.55µm (telecom wavelength) and different Lc/¯L. Assumed parameters in Table I.
